structured.summary: This paper addresses the challenge of handling the Universum class—samples that do not belong to any target class—in NLP classification tasks. The authors propose a closed boundary learning method that models target classes with closed arbitrary-shaped boundaries and treats the Universum class as the space outside these boundaries, improving classification performance across multiple NLP tasks.

strengths.point: Identification and formalization of the Universum class problem in NLP classification tasks.
strengths.evidence: Section 1 Introduction and Section 2.1 discuss the overlooked nature of the Universum class and its distinct properties such as heterogeneity and lack of representativeness.

strengths.point: Novel closed boundary learning framework that generates arbitrary-shaped boundaries for target classes.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.4 details the use of Gaussian Mixture Models to form arbitrary closed boundaries, generalizing beyond spherical boundaries.

strengths.point: Joint probability estimation strategy for the Universum class that avoids reliance on its intrinsic sample distribution.
strengths.evidence: Section 3.5 proposes a probability estimation method that respects the unique properties of the Universum class and enables joint classification.

strengths.point: Comprehensive empirical evaluation demonstrating consistent improvements across six state-of-the-art models and three NLP tasks.
strengths.evidence: Section 4.3 and Table 2 show performance gains in accuracy and F1 scores on relation extraction, sentiment analysis, and named entity recognition datasets.

weaknesses.point: Limited discussion on computational overhead and scalability of the closed boundary learning method.
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not provide analysis or benchmarks regarding training or inference time impacts of the proposed method.

weaknesses.point: Lack of ablation studies isolating the contributions of individual components such as the arbitrary shape boundaries versus the boundary learning loss.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 4.5 only ablates the N-pair loss pretraining but does not dissect other components of the method.

weaknesses.point: Potential sensitivity to hyperparameters like the number of Gaussian components and threshold values is not thoroughly explored.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 3.4 and 4.6 mention these parameters but do not provide detailed sensitivity analysis.

weaknesses.point: The method assumes availability of labeled Universum samples during training, which may not always be feasible.
weaknesses.evidence: Section 2.2.1 contrasts the problem setting with open-world assumptions where OOD samples are unavailable, but practical limitations of requiring Universum labels are not discussed.

weaknesses.point: The approach may be less effective if Universum samples are highly overlapping with target class distributions.
weaknesses.evidence: While heterogeneity is discussed, no experiments or analysis are presented on cases with ambiguous or overlapping Universum and target class samples.

weaknesses.point: No comparison with alternative methods specifically designed for handling 'other' or miscellaneous classes in NLP.
weaknesses.evidence: Related work section focuses on OOD and open set recognition but lacks direct comparison with other NLP-specific approaches for the Universum class.

weaknesses.point: The paper does not provide qualitative analysis or visualization of learned boundaries to illustrate interpretability.
weaknesses.evidence: Figures and examples focus on conceptual illustrations but no empirical visualization of decision boundaries is included.

questions.question: How sensitive is the performance to the choice of the number of Gaussian components in the GMM for each target class?
questions.motivation: Understanding hyperparameter sensitivity is important for practical adoption and tuning of the method.

questions.question: Can the method be extended or adapted to scenarios where Universum samples are not explicitly labeled during training?
questions.motivation: Many real-world datasets may not have explicit Universum annotations, so applicability in such settings is critical.

questions.question: Have the authors considered or tested the impact of the closed boundary learning on model calibration or uncertainty estimation?
questions.motivation: Closed boundaries may affect confidence scores and uncertainty, which are important for downstream decision-making.

questions.question: Could the authors provide visualizations of the learned closed boundaries in feature space to better understand how the method separates Universum and target classes?
questions.motivation: Visual evidence would help validate the effectiveness and interpretability of the proposed boundaries.

critique_points.point_001: Limited discussion on computational overhead and scalability of the closed boundary learning method. (The paper does not provide analysis or benchmarks regarding training or inference time impacts of the proposed method.)
critique_points.point_002: Lack of ablation studies isolating the contributions of individual components such as the arbitrary shape boundaries versus the boundary learning loss. (Section 4.5 only ablates the N-pair loss pretraining but does not dissect other components of the method.)
critique_points.point_003: Potential sensitivity to hyperparameters like the number of Gaussian components and threshold values is not thoroughly explored. (Section 3.4 and 4.6 mention these parameters but do not provide detailed sensitivity analysis.)
critique_points.point_004: The method assumes availability of labeled Universum samples during training, which may not always be feasible. (Section 2.2.1 contrasts the problem setting with open-world assumptions where OOD samples are unavailable, but practical limitations of requiring Universum labels are not discussed.)
critique_points.point_005: The approach may be less effective if Universum samples are highly overlapping with target class distributions. (While heterogeneity is discussed, no experiments or analysis are presented on cases with ambiguous or overlapping Universum and target class samples.)
critique_points.point_006: No comparison with alternative methods specifically designed for handling 'other' or miscellaneous classes in NLP. (Related work section focuses on OOD and open set recognition but lacks direct comparison with other NLP-specific approaches for the Universum class.)
critique_points.point_007: The paper does not provide qualitative analysis or visualization of learned boundaries to illustrate interpretability. (Figures and examples focus on conceptual illustrations but no empirical visualization of decision boundaries is included.)
critique_points.point_008: How sensitive is the performance to the choice of the number of Gaussian components in the GMM for each target class? (Understanding hyperparameter sensitivity is important for practical adoption and tuning of the method.)
critique_points.point_009: Can the method be extended or adapted to scenarios where Universum samples are not explicitly labeled during training? (Many real-world datasets may not have explicit Universum annotations, so applicability in such settings is critical.)
critique_points.point_010: Have the authors considered or tested the impact of the closed boundary learning on model calibration or uncertainty estimation? (Closed boundaries may affect confidence scores and uncertainty, which are important for downstream decision-making.)
critique_points.point_011: Could the authors provide visualizations of the learned closed boundaries in feature space to better understand how the method separates Universum and target classes? (Visual evidence would help validate the effectiveness and interpretability of the proposed boundaries.)
